Explanation: 1. In `make_buffer`, when `len <= 0`, the function returns NULL at line 2. This NULL value is the source of the propagation path. 2. In `write_record`, the return value of `make_buffer(len)` is assigned to `b` at line 2. If `len <= 0`, then `b` will be NULL. 3. At line 9 in `write_record`, `b->data[offset] = val` dereferences `b` without any null check. There is no conditional check between line 2 and line 9 that would verify whether `b` is NULL before dereferencing it. 4. The comments in the code mention "make_buffer() return value verified non-NULL before dereference of b" but this is just a comment (potentially misleading/false documentation) - there is no actual null check in the code. The actual code goes directly from the assignment at line 2 to the dereference at line 9 without any guard. 5. Therefore, when `len <= 0`, `make_buffer` returns NULL, this NULL is stored in `b`, and then `b->data[offset]` at line 9 causes a null pointer dereference. The path is reachable and does cause the NPD bug.

Answer: Yes
